title: Cost information
summary: 9609 AS — fixed vs variable costs, total and average cost, and why accurate cost data matters for decisions.
learningObjectives: Define and classify fixed and variable costs.
Calculate total cost and average cost.
Explain why accurate cost information is important.
Apply cost classification to business decisions.

simpleExplanation.title: Knowing what things cost
simpleExplanation.summary: Cost information splits spending into fixed overheads and variable costs that change with output, enabling pricing, profit analysis, and break-even calculations.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Running a café: rent and manager salary are fixed — you pay them whether you serve 10 or 100 customers. Coffee beans and takeaway cups are variable — the more you sell, the more you spend. You need both numbers before you can price a latte or know if a busy Saturday is actually profitable.
steps: Fixed costs: rent, salaries — unchanged by output level.
Variable costs: materials, piece-rate wages — rise with output.
Total cost = fixed costs + total variable costs.
Average cost = total cost ÷ output (units produced or sold).

flashcards.back: A cost that does not change with the level of output in the short run (e.g. rent, salaried staff, insurance).
A cost that changes in direct proportion to output (e.g. raw materials, commission, packaging).
Total cost = Fixed costs + Total variable costs.

flashcards.front: Why classify costs as fixed vs variable?
flashcards.back: Enables contribution analysis, break-even, pricing decisions, and budget control.
Electricity with a standing charge plus usage — partly fixed, partly variable.

flashcards.front: Why is accurate cost data important?
flashcards.back: Wrong costs lead to bad pricing, false profit figures, and poor investment decisions.
As output rises, average fixed cost falls (spreading overheads); average variable cost may rise if efficiency falls.

faq.q: Is labour always a fixed cost?
faq.a: No. Salaried staff are usually fixed in the short run; piece-rate or hourly casual workers paid only when producing are variable. Read the case carefully.
A cost with both fixed and variable elements (e.g. phone bill with line rental + call charges). Examiners may ask you to split or treat as one category if told.

sections.content: **Cost information** (5.4.1) is the foundation for all **9609 AS** finance calculations in unit 5.4. Before break-even, contribution, or budgeting, you must **correctly classify costs** as fixed or variable. Examiners often hide classification in a case paragraph — underline each cost and label it before calculating.

**Fixed costs (FC)** do not change when output changes in the **short run**. Examples: rent, insurance, salaried management, depreciation on existing equipment.

**Variable costs (VC)** change in proportion to output. Examples: direct materials, packaging, sales commission per unit, piece-rate wages.

Some costs are **semi-variable** (mixed): e.g. electricity with a fixed standing charge plus usage. In exams, follow the case instructions — either split the cost or treat it as given.

items: **Short run only:** Fixed costs are fixed only in the time period given — rent may be fixed this year but renegotiated next year.
**Direct vs indirect** is different from fixed vs variable — a cost can be direct (traceable to a product) and still fixed (e.g. supervisor salary for one product line).
**Classify before calculating** — mislabelling a variable cost as fixed destroys break-even and contribution answers.

sections.content:
Total cost (TC) = Fixed costs + Total variable costs

Average cost (AC) = $\frac{\text{Total cost}}{\text{Output (units)}}$

Average fixed cost (AFC) = $\frac{\text{Fixed costs}}{\text{Output}}$

Average variable cost (AVC) = $\frac{\text{Total variable costs}}{\text{Output}}$

As output increases, **average fixed cost falls** (fixed costs spread over more units). **Average variable cost** may stay constant or rise if, for example, overtime or bulk discounts change unit costs. **Average total cost** typically falls then may rise — the familiar U-shape in theory, though 9609 focuses on calculation not drawing AC curves.

sections.question: A workshop has fixed costs of $8 000 per month. Variable cost is $12 per unit. Calculate total cost and average cost when output is (a) 500 units, (b) 1 000 units.
sections.solution:
**At 500 units**
Total variable cost = 500 × $12 = $6 000
Total cost = $8 000 + $6 000 = **$14 000**
Average cost = $14 000 ÷ 500 = **$28.00** per unit

**At 1 000 units**
Total variable cost = 1 000 × $12 = $12 000
Total cost = $8 000 + $12 000 = **$20 000**
Average cost = $20 000 ÷ 1 000 = **$20.00** per unit

Average cost falls because fixed costs are spread over more units (AFC falls from $16 to $8).

sections.content: Managers use cost data to **set prices**, **measure profit by product**, **control spending**, and **plan production**. Inaccurate data — missing overheads, wrong classification, outdated material prices — leads to selling below cost, overstated profit, and poor decisions.
In case studies, list costs from the text in a table (Cost | Fixed/Variable | $ amount) before any calculation. Examiners reward clear classification even when the final number is wrong.
